content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: My bag is ___ than yours — it has three heavy books inside.
distractor_rationale: one-syllable adjectives form the comparative with -er, not 'more'
double comparative is always wrong — never use 'more' with an -er form
superlative 'heaviest' compares more than two items; here only two bags are compared

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom's handwriting is ___ than his sister's — you can read every letter clearly.
distractor_rationale: one-syllable adjectives take -er for the comparative, not 'more'
double comparative combining 'more' and '-er' is always incorrect
superlative form is used for three or more items, not a two-way comparison

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The weather today is ___ than it was yesterday, so we decided to go for a walk.
distractor_rationale: 'good' has an irregular comparative form; 'more good' is a common but incorrect construction
'gooder' is not a real English word; 'good' does not take the -er suffix
'best' is the superlative of 'good' and cannot be used in a two-item comparison with 'than'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The new apartment is ___ expensive ___ the old one, but it is much closer to the office.
distractor_rationale: 'that' cannot follow a comparative adjective; the correct conjunction is 'than'
'fewer' is used with countable nouns, not adjectives like 'expensive'
'not as expensive that' is grammatically incorrect; the correct structure is 'not as … as'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Sara speaks English ___ fluently ___ her brother, even though he studied abroad for a year.
distractor_rationale: 'more fluently that' uses the wrong conjunction 'that' instead of 'than' after a comparative
'as fluently that' uses the wrong conjunction; the as…as structure requires 'as' on both sides
'most fluently' is a superlative adverb and cannot be used in an equality comparison with 'as'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The second test was ___ than the first one, so many students felt relieved when they saw their results.
distractor_rationale: two-syllable adjectives ending in -y drop the -y and add -er, not 'more'
double comparative mixing 'more' and '-er' is always ungrammatical
superlative 'easiest' cannot be used with 'than' in a two-item comparison

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The traffic on the main road was ___ than usual this morning, so James arrived at the meeting late.
distractor_rationale: 'bad' has an irregular comparative; 'more bad' is a frequent learner error but is always incorrect
double comparative 'more worse' combines 'more' with the already comparative form 'worse', which is ungrammatical
'worst' is the superlative of 'bad' and requires no 'than' in a two-item comparison

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: ___ carefully you read the instructions before assembling the furniture, ___ mistakes you will make.
distractor_rationale: the double comparative structure requires 'the' before each comparative element; omitting 'the' breaks the construction
'the less' is used with uncountable nouns or adjectives, but 'mistakes' is a countable noun requiring 'fewer'
superlative forms 'most' and 'fewest' cannot be used in the the more…the more double comparative structure

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: ___ time you spend practising the piano each day, ___ progress you will notice at your weekly lesson.
distractor_rationale: the double comparative structure requires the definite article 'the' before each comparative; omitting it produces an ungrammatical result
superlative forms cannot be used in the double comparative the more…the more structure
'the much more' is ungrammatical; 'much' can modify a comparative but cannot combine with 'the more' in this fixed structure

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The new software updates the database ___ quickly ___ the old system did, which saves the team a lot of time every day.
distractor_rationale: multiplier comparisons use 'as…as', not 'more…than'; 'twice more quickly than' is a common but incorrect blend of two different structures
'that' cannot replace the second 'as' in an as…as equality structure; the conjunction is always 'as'
'two times more quickly as' incorrectly mixes the multiplier 'more…than' pattern with the 'as' conjunction, producing an ungrammatical hybrid

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: My bag is ___ than yours — I can barely lift it!
distractor_rationale: one-syllable adjectives ending in a consonant take -er, not 'more'; 'more heavy' is a double-marking error
'more heavier' is a double comparative error combining both -er and more simultaneously
'heaviest' is the superlative used for three or more items, not a comparison between two things

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The soup today tastes ___ than the soup we had last week.
distractor_rationale: 'more good' uses the wrong irregular comparative; 'good' has the irregular form 'better'
'gooder' is a non-existent form; 'good' does not take the regular -er ending
'best' is the superlative of 'good' and is used when comparing three or more items, not two

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The new office building is ___ modern ___ the old one near the train station.
distractor_rationale: 'that' is a conjunction used in relative clauses and reported speech, not after comparatives; the correct word is 'than'
'most' forms the superlative and requires 'the most … of all', not a two-item comparison with 'than'
'as … than' is a mixed construction; the correct paired structure is either 'as … as' for equality or 'more … than' for superiority

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: James finished the report ___ quickly as his colleague, so they both left the office at the same time.
distractor_rationale: 'more quickly' requires 'than' to complete the comparison and signals superiority, but the second clause confirms equal speed, making superiority impossible
'than' must follow a comparative form such as 'more quickly'; it cannot directly follow 'quickly' alone to express equality
'less quickly … as' is not a grammatical construction; 'less' requires 'than', and the equal-time result in the second clause contradicts inferiority

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: ___ more time you spend practising your pronunciation every day, ___ more confident you will feel when speaking to native speakers.
distractor_rationale: 'A … a' does not form the double comparative structure; the fixed pattern requires the definite article 'the' before each comparative clause
'More … more' without 'the' breaks the grammatical pattern; the double comparative in English requires 'the more … the more', not bare 'more … more'
'The … a' uses an inconsistent article pairing; both clauses in the double comparative must begin with 'the' to form a parallel, grammatically correct structure

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: My bag is ___ than yours.
distractor_rationale: one-syllable and two-syllable adjectives ending in -y take -er, not 'more'
'heaviest' is the superlative form, used for three or more items, not for comparing two
double comparative combining 'more' and '-er' is a common learner error — only one form is correct

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom runs ___ than his brother does.
distractor_rationale: 'fast' is a one-syllable adverb that takes -er, not 'more'
'fastest' is the superlative, used when comparing three or more, not two people
double comparative 'more faster' combines both forms incorrectly — only one is used

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: This coffee is ___ than the one I had this morning.
distractor_rationale: 'more good' is a common error — 'good' has the irregular comparative form 'better'
'gooder' applies a regular -er ending to an irregular adjective, which is incorrect
'best' is the superlative of 'good' and cannot be used when comparing just two things

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The new apartment is more expensive ___ the old one, but it has a much bigger kitchen.
distractor_rationale: 'that' is a very common learner error — the correct conjunction after a comparative is 'than', not 'that'
'as' is used in 'as…as' equality structures, not after a comparative adjective
'from' is used in 'different from', not after comparative adjectives

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Anna speaks English ___ as her classmate, so they were placed in the same group.
distractor_rationale: 'more fluently' signals a difference in ability, but the sentence says they were placed in the same group, indicating equal ability
'fluently than' is the structure for inequality comparatives, which contradicts the equal placement context
'the most fluently' is a superlative adverb form used for three or more, not for expressing equality between two

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The second film in the series was ___ than the first one — I nearly fell asleep.
distractor_rationale: 'more boring than' includes an extra 'than' making the sentence grammatically incorrect in this position
'the least exciting' is a superlative used for three or more items, not a comparison of two films
'fewer' is used with countable nouns, not with adjectives; 'less' is the correct comparative form here

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The weather today is ___ than it was yesterday, so we decided to go for a walk in the park.
distractor_rationale: 'more warm' incorrectly uses 'more' with a short one-syllable adjective that takes -er
'most warm' is a superlative form used for three or more comparisons, not two days
'more warmer' is a double comparative error combining both 'more' and '-er' on the same adjective

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: ___ you practise speaking a foreign language, ___ you become at using it naturally.
distractor_rationale: 'More…more' omits the required definite article 'the' before each comparative in the double comparative structure
'The most…the best' incorrectly uses superlative forms in the double comparative structure, which requires comparative forms
'As more…as better' confuses the 'as…as' equality structure with the double comparative and combines them incorrectly

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: David said the second route through the mountains was ___ than the first, so we chose it to save time.
distractor_rationale: 'more safe' is not wrong in isolation, but the sentence context — saving time by choosing the quicker route — implies the speaker is framing it as reducing danger, and 'more safe' would require 'safer'; more critically, 'more safe' is unnatural compared to 'safer' or 'less dangerous' in standard usage here
'lesser dangerous' misuses 'lesser', which is a fixed adjective meaning 'of smaller importance' and cannot be used as a comparative adverb modifying another adjective
'fewer dangerous' incorrectly uses 'fewer', which modifies countable nouns, not adjectives

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: ___ the team trained together, ___ their performance became during the final weeks of the season.
distractor_rationale: 'the most consistent' uses a superlative in the second clause, but double comparatives require comparative forms in both clauses
omitting 'the' before both comparatives breaks the fixed 'the more…the more' double comparative structure
'as consistent' belongs to the 'as…as' equality structure and cannot replace the second comparative in a double comparative pattern

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: My bag is more heavy that your bag.
content.error_sentence: My bag is more heavy that your bag.
content.corrected_sentence: My bag is more heavy than your bag.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: This coffee is more good than the one I had yesterday.
content.error_sentence: This coffee is more good than the one I had yesterday.
content.corrected_sentence: This coffee is better than the one I had yesterday.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The new sports centre is more bigger than the old one in the town centre.
content.error_sentence: The new sports centre is more bigger than the old one in the town centre.
content.corrected_sentence: The new sports centre is bigger than the old one in the town centre.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Sara's results in the maths test were more bad than her results last term.
content.error_sentence: Sara's results in the maths test were more bad than her results last term.
content.corrected_sentence: Sara's results in the maths test were worse than her results last term.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The journey by train is not as quickly as the journey by plane, but it is much cheaper.
content.error_sentence: The journey by train is not as quickly as the journey by plane, but it is much cheaper.
content.corrected_sentence: The journey by train is not as quick as the journey by plane, but it is much cheaper.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Between the two apartments, the one near the park is the most affordable.
content.error_sentence: Between the two apartments, the one near the park is the most affordable.
content.corrected_sentence: Between the two apartments, the one near the park is more affordable.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The more faster you drive on wet roads, the more dangerous your journey becomes.
content.error_sentence: The more faster you drive on wet roads, the more dangerous your journey becomes.
content.corrected_sentence: The faster you drive on wet roads, the more dangerous your journey becomes.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The more time David spent practising the piano, the more better his performances became at the school concerts.
content.error_sentence: The more time David spent practising the piano, the more better his performances became at the school concerts.
content.corrected_sentence: The more time David spent practising the piano, the better his performances became at the school concerts.
